sub_groups.name: Escapes to David from the vengeance of Saul, who slew the priests in the City of Nob 1Sa 22:20-23; with 22:6-19
Associate high priest with Zadok in the reign of David 2Sa 15:35; 20:25; 1Ki 4:4; 1Ch 15:11
But called AHIMELECH and father of Abiathar 2Sa 8:17; 1Ch 18:16
Loyal to David when Absalom rebelled; leaves Jerusalem with the ark of the covenant, but is directed by David to return with the ark 2Sa 15:24-29
Helps David by sending his son from Jerusalem to David with secret information concerning the counsel of Ahithophel 2Sa 15:35,36; 17:15-22; 1Ki 2:26
Supports Adonijah’s pretensions to the throne 1Ki 1:7
Thrust out of office by Solomon 1Ki 2:26,27; with 1Sa 2:31-35

ai_enrichment.summary: Abiathar, filho de Ahimelech, foi o décimo sumo sacerdote de Israel e um importante aliado de Davi. Após a morte de seu pai, ele fugiu para Davi, levando consigo o efóde, e se tornou um sacerdote durante o reinado de Davi. Abiathar permaneceu leal a Davi durante suas dificuldades, incluindo a rebelião de Absalão. No entanto, ele foi deposto por Salomão por apoiar Adonias na luta pelo trono, marcando a única deposição de um sumo sacerdote na história de Israel. Sua história ilustra temas de lealdade, traição e a transição de poder no sacerdócio israelita.
outline: 1. Origem e significado do nome Abiathar: 'pai da abundância' e sua linhagem como filho de Ahimelech, o sacerdote (1 Samuel 22:20-23).
2. Fuga para Davi: Abiathar escapa do massacre dos sacerdotes em Nob e se junta a Davi na caverna de Adulão, trazendo o efóde (1 Samuel 22:20-21).
3. Papel como sacerdote: Abiathar serve como sacerdote durante o reinado de Davi, consultando o Senhor em momentos críticos (1 Samuel 30:7; 2 Samuel 2:1).
4. Lealdade a Davi: Sua fidelidade a Davi durante a rebelião de Absalão e seu papel como conselheiro (2 Samuel 15:24-29).
5. Deposição e consequências: A deposição de Abiathar por Salomão devido ao seu apoio a Adonias, resultando na transferência do sacerdócio para Zadok (1 Reis 2:26-27).
Role of Abiathar in David's reign (2 Samuel 15:24-29; 1 Samuel 30:7)
Consequences of political alliances (1 Reis 2:26-27; 1 Crônicas 27:34)
The significance of the ephod (1 Samuel 22:20-23; 1 Samuel 30:7)
ai_enrichment.theological_significance: A história de Abiathar destaca a importância do sacerdócio em Israel e a relação entre liderança espiritual e política. Sua lealdade a Davi e a subsequente deposição por Salomão ilustram as complexidades do poder e a necessidade de fidelidade a Deus em tempos de crise. A narrativa também reflete a soberania de Deus na escolha de líderes e na preservação de Seu povo.

ai_themes_normalized.aspect: dinâmica de lealdade e traição entre líderes
importância da consulta divina nas decisões

original_theme.label_original: A consulta a Deus em decisões críticas

definitions.text: Father of abundance, or my father excels, the son of Ahimelech the high priest. He was the tenth high priest, and the fourth in descent from Eli. When his father was slain with the priests of Nob, he escaped, and bearing with him the ephod, he joined David, who was then in the cave of Adullam (1 Sam. 22:20-23; 23:6). He remained with David, and became priest of the party of which he was the leader (1 Sam. 30:7). When David ascended the throne of Judah, Abiathar was appointed high priest (1 Chr. 15:11; 1 Kings 2:26) and the “king’s companion” (1 Chr. 27:34). Meanwhile Zadok, of the house of Eleazar, had been made high priest. These appointments continued in force till the end of David’s reign (1 Kings 4:4). Abiathar was deposed (the sole historical instance of the deposition of a high priest) and banished to his home at Anathoth by Solomon, because he took part in the attempt to raise Adonijah to the throne. The priesthood thus passed from the house of Ithamar (1 Sam. 2:30-36; 1 Kings 1:19; 2:26, 27). Zadok now became sole high priest. In Mark 2:26, reference is made to an occurrence in “the days of Abiathar the high priest.” But from 1 Sam. 22, we learn explicitly that this event took place when Ahimelech, the father of Abiathar, was high priest. The apparent discrepancy is satisfactorily explained by interpreting the words in Mark as referring to the life-time of Abiathar, and not to the term of his holding the office of high priest. It is not implied in Mark that he was actual high priest at the time referred to. Others, however, think that the loaves belonged to Abiathar, who was at that time (Lev. 24:9) a priest, and that he either himself gave them to David, or persuaded his father to give them.

(father of abundance, i.e. liberal), High priest and fourth in descent from Eli. (B.C. 1060-1012.) Abiathar was the only one of the all the sons of Ahimelech the high priest who escaped the slaughter inflicted upon his father’s house by Saul, in revenge for his father’s house by Saul, in revenge of his having inquired of the Lord for David and given him the shew-bread to eat. (1 Samuel 22:1) ... Abiathar having become high priest fled to David, and was thus enabled to inquire of the Lord for him. (1 Samuel 23:9; 30:7; 2 Samuel 2:1; 5:19) etc. He adhered to David in his wanderings while pursued by Saul; he was with him while he reigned in Hebron, and afterwards in Jerusalem. (2 Samuel 2:1-3) He continued faithful to him in Absalom’s rebellion. (2 Samuel 15:24,29,35,36; 17:15-17; 19:11) When, however, Adonijah set himself up fro David’s successor on the throne, in opposition to Solomon, Abiathar sided with him, while Zadok was on Solomon’s side. For this Abiathar was deprived of the high priesthood. Zadok had joined David at Hebron, (1 Chronicles 12:28) so that there was henceforth who high priests in the reign of David, and till the deposition of Abiathar by Solomon, when Zadok became the sole high priest.

ai_discovered_aspects.label: Role of Abiathar in David's reign
ai_discovered_aspects.rationale: Abiathar's role as a priest and advisor during critical moments in David's reign illustrates the intersection of spiritual leadership and political authority.

ai_discovered_aspects.label: Consequences of political alliances
ai_discovered_aspects.rationale: The political alliances and betrayals surrounding Abiathar's deposition highlight the complexities of leadership and loyalty in ancient Israel.

ai_discovered_aspects.label: The significance of the ephod
ai_discovered_aspects.rationale: The ephod's role in divine consultation emphasizes the importance of seeking God's guidance in leadership decisions.

ai_relationships.description: Abiathar foi um aliado leal de Davi durante sua ascensão ao trono.
Abiathar se opôs a Salomão ao apoiar Adonias, resultando em sua deposição.
Zadok sucedeu Abiathar como sumo sacerdote após sua deposição.
Abiathar é filho de Ahimelech, o sumo sacerdote assassinado por Saul.
